learning_objectives: Understand the role of QA within the game development lifecycle
Distinguish between different types of game testing
Recognize how QA processes differ between games and traditional software
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 1-3
ISTQB Foundation Level Syllabus - Testing Fundamentals
Gamasutra: The Role of QA in Modern Game Development

lecture_notes.introduction: Quality assurance in games is not just about finding bugs. It is about ensuring the player experience meets design intent across thousands of hardware configurations, input methods, and play styles. A single crash during a boss fight can cost you a player forever and a negative review that discourages hundreds more.
key_concepts: QA in the software development lifecycle: waterfall vs agile vs game-specific workflows
Types of game testing: functional, compliance, compatibility, localization, performance, accessibility
The cost of defects: why bugs found in production cost 10-100x more to fix than bugs found in development
QA team structures: embedded QA, centralized QA, outsourced QA
The distinction between QA (process) and testing (activity)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a real shipped game's public bug database to analyze bug severity distribution, examining how critical vs minor bugs were triaged, and discussing which bugs should have been caught earlier in the pipeline
setup_requirements: Bug tracking tool access (Jira, Azure DevOps, or equivalent)
Sample game project with intentional defects for testing exercises
Screen recording software for bug reproduction documentation

review.value: Critical (crash, data loss, blocks progression), Major (significant feature broken but workaround exists), Minor (cosmetic issue, minor inconvenience), Trivial (typo, alignment off by pixels); severity determines fix priority and whether a build ships

Moving testing activities earlier in the development cycle rather than waiting for a complete build; includes code reviews, unit tests, and design reviews; each defect caught earlier saves exponentially more time and money

class_schedule.topic: Test Plan Creation and Bug Reporting
learning_objectives: Write comprehensive test plans for game features
Document bugs with sufficient detail for reliable reproduction
Organize test cases into logical suites with priority and coverage tracking
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 5-7
Best Practices for Bug Reporting in Game Development

lecture_notes.introduction: A test plan is a contract between QA and development that defines what will be tested, how, and what constitutes pass/fail. A bug report is a communication tool that must enable a developer to reproduce the issue without asking a single follow-up question. Both are professional documents that reflect on the quality of the QA team.
key_concepts: Test plan components: scope, approach, resources, schedule, entry/exit criteria
Test case design: preconditions, steps, expected results, actual results
Bug report anatomy: summary, severity, priority, reproduction steps, expected vs actual, environment, attachments
Equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis for input testing
Test case management tools and traceability matrices
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a test plan for a game's inventory system, creating 15 test cases covering normal operation, edge cases, and error conditions, then filing a properly formatted bug report with video capture, system info, and reproduction steps
setup_requirements: Jira or equivalent bug tracking tool with game QA workflow configured
Test case management tool (TestRail, qTest, or spreadsheet template)
Screen recording software with annotation capability
Sample game build with inventory system

review.value: The fraction of attempts that successfully trigger the bug, expressed as X/Y (e.g., 3/5 means 3 out of 5 attempts); a 1/1 bug is reliably reproducible while a 1/50 bug is intermittent and much harder to fix; always include this in bug reports

Testing at the exact boundaries of valid input ranges and one step beyond; for an inventory with 99 max slots, test at 0, 1, 98, 99, and 100 items; most bugs cluster at boundaries where code transitions between conditions

learning_objectives: Design and execute regression test suites for game builds
Implement automated tests for game systems using testing frameworks
Integrate automated tests into a continuous integration pipeline
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 9-10
Continuous Integration for Games (GDC 2023 Talk Notes)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every bug fix risks introducing a new bug. Regression testing verifies that previously working features still work after code changes. Done manually, this is the most tedious and expensive part of QA. Automation transforms regression from a bottleneck into a safety net that runs on every commit.
key_concepts: Regression testing strategy: full regression vs risk-based selective regression
Smoke testing: the minimum set of tests that verify a build is stable enough for further testing
Unity Test Framework: Edit Mode tests for logic, Play Mode tests for runtime behavior
Test automation pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, minimal UI tests
CI/CD integration: running automated tests on every pull request with build gating
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing automated tests for a game's combat damage system: unit tests for damage calculation, integration tests for the damage-health-death pipeline, and a Play Mode test that spawns an enemy, applies damage, and verifies the death animation triggers. Then configuring GitHub Actions to run these tests on every push.
setup_requirements: Unity project with Test Framework package installed
Sample game with combat system for testing
GitHub repository with Actions enabled
Unity Build Server license or Unity Cloud Build access

review.value: A small, fast set of tests (typically 10-30 minutes) that verify the build boots, loads the main menu, can enter gameplay, and the core loop functions; if smoke tests fail, the build is rejected without wasting time on deeper testing

Base: many fast unit tests for individual functions (seconds to run); middle: fewer integration tests that verify system interactions (minutes); top: minimal end-to-end tests that simulate real gameplay (tens of minutes); inverting the pyramid leads to slow, brittle, expensive test suites

learning_objectives: Profile game performance to identify CPU, GPU, and memory bottlenecks
Execute compatibility testing across hardware configurations and operating system versions
Establish performance budgets and create automated performance regression detection
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 12-13
Performance Testing Guidance for Web Applications (adapted for games)

lecture_notes.introduction: A game that runs perfectly on your development machine may run terribly on your players' hardware. Performance testing and compatibility testing ensure the game delivers an acceptable experience across the range of supported configurations, not just the best-case scenario.
key_concepts: CPU profiling: identifying hot functions, GC allocation spikes, main thread vs job thread balance
GPU profiling: draw call overhead, fill rate limits, shader complexity, overdraw visualization
Memory profiling: managed heap growth, native memory leaks, texture memory budgets
Compatibility matrix: OS versions, GPU vendors, driver versions, screen resolutions, input devices
Automated performance benchmarks: capturing frame time percentiles across standardized gameplay sequences
lecture_notes.demonstration: Profiling a sample game that runs at 60fps on a high-end machine but drops to 25fps on a mid-range laptop. Using Unity Profiler to identify a GC allocation spike in the update loop, a GPU overdraw issue in particle effects, and a memory leak from pooled objects not being returned. Fixing each issue and verifying the fix on both hardware tiers.
setup_requirements: Unity Profiler and Frame Debugger
Two machines with different hardware specifications for comparison testing
Sample game build with intentional performance issues
Spreadsheet template for compatibility test matrix

review.value: Average FPS hides stutters; the 99th percentile frame time reveals the worst 1% of frames that players actually feel as hitches; a game with 60fps average but 50ms P99 will feel stuttery despite the good average

When the garbage collector runs to reclaim unused managed memory, it pauses the game thread for milliseconds; caused by allocating temporary objects every frame (string concatenation, LINQ queries, lambda closures); prevent by pre-allocating and reusing objects

learning_objectives: Design and conduct structured playtesting sessions
Collect and analyze qualitative and quantitative player feedback
Distinguish between usability testing, focus testing, and balance testing
readings: The Art of Game Design Ch. 26 - Playtesting
Quantitative Methods in Games User Research (GDC 2022)

lecture_notes.introduction: Playtesting is where QA meets design. Technical QA finds bugs in the code; playtesting finds bugs in the design. A feature can work perfectly from a technical standpoint and still be confusing, frustrating, or boring. You cannot discover this by staring at code - you discover it by watching real players.
key_concepts: Usability testing: can the player figure out what to do and how to do it?
Focus testing: do players enjoy the experience and want to continue playing?
Balance testing: are difficulty curves, progression rates, and competitive matchups fair?
Think-aloud protocol: having players verbalize their thought process during play
Quantitative analytics: heatmaps, funnel analysis, session length distribution, rage quit detection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running a live moderated playtest session: briefing the participant, conducting think-aloud observation without leading the player, taking timestamped notes, administering a post-session questionnaire, and analyzing the combined qualitative and quantitative data to produce three prioritized design recommendations
setup_requirements: Playable game build at a testable milestone
Screen and face recording setup (OBS with camera overlay)
Playtesting consent form and questionnaire templates
Observation note-taking template with timestamp column

review.value: Players behave differently when they know they are being watched; mitigate by making the player comfortable, emphasizing you are testing the game not the player, using one-way mirrors or remote observation, and letting the player settle in for 5 minutes before taking notes

Identifying when a player quits out of frustration rather than boredom or time constraints; indicators include repeated deaths at the same point, increasingly short session times, sudden quit after a failure, and aggressive input patterns immediately before exit

learning_objectives: Understand platform certification requirements for console, PC storefronts, and mobile app stores
Prepare a game for certification submission with compliance checklists
Identify and resolve common certification failure reasons before submission
readings: Game Testing: All in One Ch. 15-16

lecture_notes.introduction: Before a game reaches players on any major platform, it must pass certification. Each platform holder has hundreds of specific requirements covering everything from save data behavior during power loss to button prompt accuracy. Failing certification delays your launch by weeks and costs tens of thousands of dollars in resubmission fees. QA's job is to ensure the game passes on the first attempt.
key_concepts: Console certification (XR/TRC/Lotcheck): mandatory requirements for Xbox, PlayStation, Nintendo
PC storefront requirements: Steam, Epic Games Store, GOG compliance and content guidelines
Mobile certification: Apple App Store Review Guidelines, Google Play policies
Common certification failures: incorrect button prompts, save data corruption on power loss, network disconnection handling, accessibility minimums
Compliance testing workflow: internal cert pass, pre-certification checklist, submission, response handling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing an internal certification pass on a sample game build using a simplified version of Xbox Requirements: verifying correct controller button prompts match the active input device, testing save data integrity after simulated power loss, verifying the game handles network disconnection gracefully during online gameplay, and checking that all user-generated content has appropriate reporting mechanisms
setup_requirements: Sample game build targeting a console-equivalent platform
Platform certification requirements checklist (simplified for course use)
Multiple input devices for button prompt verification
Network simulation tools for disconnection testing

review.value: Technical Certification Requirements (PlayStation), Xbox Requirements, and Nintendo Lotcheck are the formal certification processes each console manufacturer requires; covering hundreds of rules about system integration, error handling, save data, and user experience that must all pass before a game is approved for sale on the platform

Verifying that game save files remain valid after interrupted writes (power loss, crash, storage full); typically requires implementing atomic writes using a temporary file that is renamed after completion, plus keeping a backup of the previous save

quizzes.topic: QA Methodology, Test Planning, and Bug Reporting

questions.question: A tester files the following bug report: 'The game crashes sometimes when I open the inventory.' Identify four specific improvements that would make this bug report actionable for a developer, and rewrite the summary line following professional bug reporting conventions.
questions.answer: Four improvements needed: (1) Add exact reproduction steps with numbered sequence (e.g., Step 1: Start new game, Step 2: Pick up 15+ items, Step 3: Open inventory with Tab key). (2) Include reproduction rate (e.g., 3/10 attempts). (3) Specify the environment: OS version, hardware specs, game build number, and any relevant settings. (4) Attach evidence: crash log file, video of the crash occurring, and a screenshot of any error dialog. Rewritten summary: '[Crash] Game crashes to desktop when opening inventory with 15+ items (3/10 repro, Build 2024.1.15, Windows 11)'.

rubric.key: Identifies need for specific reproduction steps
Identifies need for reproduction rate and environment info
Rewritten summary is specific and follows conventions

questions.question: Explain the difference between a smoke test suite and a full regression test suite. When would you run each, and what is the consequence of skipping smoke testing on a new build?
questions.answer: A smoke test suite is a small set of tests (10-30 minutes) that verifies the most critical functionality: the game launches, reaches the main menu, can start a new game, and the core gameplay loop functions without crashes. A full regression suite comprehensively retests all features and previously fixed bugs, often taking hours or days. Smoke tests are run on every new build before any other testing begins. Full regression is run at major milestones, before releases, and after significant merges. If you skip smoke testing on a new build and proceed directly to full regression or feature testing, testers may waste hours testing a fundamentally broken build (e.g., one that crashes on startup or has a corrupted data file), only to have all their results invalidated when the build is rejected.

rubric.key: Correct definition of smoke test with scope and timing
Correct definition of full regression with scope and timing
Clear consequence of skipping smoke tests

questions.question: A game has an average frame rate of 58fps but players complain it feels 'stuttery.' The developer insists the performance is fine because the average is near 60fps. Explain what metric the developer should examine instead of average FPS, and describe what it would likely reveal in this scenario.
questions.answer: The developer should examine frame time percentiles, specifically the 99th percentile (P99) frame time and the frame time variance. Average FPS of 58 could mask severe inconsistency: for example, 95% of frames might render in 10ms (100fps) while 5% spike to 80ms (12.5fps) due to garbage collection pauses or asset streaming hitches. The P99 frame time would reveal these spikes that players perceive as stutters. The developer should also look at a frame time graph over time to identify periodic patterns (suggesting GC allocation cycles) or situational spikes (suggesting specific gameplay events triggering expensive operations). A stuttery 58fps average likely has a P99 above 30ms and periodic frame time spikes that coincide with specific game events.

rubric.key: Identifies P99 frame time as the correct metric
Explains how average FPS masks spikes
Describes what the metric would likely reveal in this case

questions.question: You are conducting a moderated playtesting session and the player gets stuck on a puzzle for 5 minutes. They look frustrated but have not asked for help. As the facilitator, describe the correct approach and explain why you should not simply tell them the solution.
questions.answer: The correct approach is to continue observing without intervening. Ask neutral prompting questions like 'What are you thinking right now?' or 'What do you think you need to do?' to elicit their thought process through think-aloud protocol. If they are genuinely stuck and becoming too frustrated to continue productively, you may gently redirect with 'Have you tried interacting with anything else in the room?' rather than giving the solution. You should not tell them the solution because: (1) The point of playtesting is to discover where the design fails to communicate, not to prove the puzzle is solvable. If you give the solution, you lose the data about why they got stuck. (2) Once you teach the player, you can never test that moment again - this participant is contaminated for this puzzle. (3) The frustration itself is data: if players consistently get stuck here, the puzzle needs clearer signposting, not smarter players.

rubric.key: Describes non-leading observation and neutral prompting
Explains that the stuck moment is valuable data about design failure
Notes that giving the answer contaminates the participant

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive QA Test Plan and Bug Report Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Create a complete QA test plan for an assigned game build, execute the test plan, and produce a portfolio of professional bug reports. The test plan must cover functional, performance, and compatibility testing. You must file at least 15 bug reports of varying severity with proper reproduction steps, evidence, and classification.
objectives: Write a professional test plan covering multiple testing types
Execute test cases systematically and track pass/fail results
File bug reports that meet industry standards for clarity and completeness
Perform basic performance profiling and document findings
deliverables: Test plan document with scope, approach, test cases, and schedule (5-8 pages)
Test execution report with pass/fail results for all test cases
Minimum 15 bug reports filed in the tracking tool with screenshots or video
Performance profiling summary with frame time data and identified bottlenecks

criteria: Test plan covers functional, performance, and compatibility areas
Test cases have clear preconditions, steps, and expected results
Edge cases and boundary conditions are included
Entry and exit criteria are defined
Each report has precise reproduction steps a developer can follow
Severity and priority are correctly assigned and justified
Evidence is attached (screenshots, video, logs) for every report
Reports cover a range of severity levels and feature areas
All test cases have documented results
Failed test cases have corresponding bug reports
Test coverage is systematic, not random exploration
Retesting of fixed bugs is documented
Documents are well-organized and clearly written
Bug reports use consistent formatting throughout
Technical language is used accurately
Work demonstrates thoroughness and attention to detail
Test plan is comprehensive enough to ship a game against
Bug reports are developer-ready with no follow-up questions needed
Test execution is systematic with full traceability
Professional quality documents suitable for a portfolio

major_assignments.title: Automated Test Suite and CI Pipeline
major_assignments.description: Build an automated test suite for an assigned game project that covers unit tests, integration tests, and at least one end-to-end Play Mode test. Integrate the test suite into a CI pipeline that runs on every push and blocks merges on test failure. Include a performance benchmark test that detects frame time regressions.
objectives: Write automated unit tests for game logic systems
Create integration tests that verify system interactions
Implement a Play Mode test that validates runtime behavior
Configure a CI pipeline that gates merges on test results
deliverables: Unity project with test assemblies for Edit Mode and Play Mode tests
Minimum 20 unit tests, 5 integration tests, and 2 Play Mode tests
GitHub Actions workflow file that runs all tests on push
Documentation describing test strategy and coverage rationale (2-3 pages)

criteria: Tests cover core game logic (damage, scoring, inventory, etc.)
Edge cases and error conditions are tested
Tests are isolated with proper mocking of dependencies
Tests run in under 30 seconds total
Integration tests verify correct interaction between systems
Play Mode tests validate runtime behavior in a scene
Tests are reliable and do not flake on repeated runs
Performance benchmark test detects regressions above threshold
Pipeline runs all tests on every push to main and PR branches
Failed tests block the merge with clear error reporting
Pipeline completes in under 15 minutes
Test results are visible in pull request status checks
Test strategy explains what is tested and why
Coverage gaps are acknowledged with justification
CI pipeline setup is documented for team onboarding
Professional writing and formatting
Automated tests catch real bugs, not just exercise code paths
CI pipeline is a genuine quality gate that prevents regressions
Test suite runs fast and reliably without flakes
Documentation enables another developer to maintain the suite

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of QA methodology, testing theory, and industry practices

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in test case writing, bug reporting, profiling, and playtesting

major_assignments.description: Two major projects covering manual QA documentation and automated testing with CI integration

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive assessment of QA processes, testing types, and bug classification

final_project.description: Full QA pass on a class game project: test plan, execution, automation, and certification readiness report

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions and playtesting workshops. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure. Playtesting sessions require advance scheduling and cannot be rescheduled.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement. Bug report portfolios submitted late miss the grading window and receive a maximum of 70%.
course_policies.collaboration: Test plans and bug reports must be individually authored. Students may share game builds for testing but all documentation, analysis, and code must be original work. Playtesting partners must be credited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All test code must be original or properly attributed. Copying bug reports from public databases or other students will result in course failure. AI-generated test cases must be disclosed and manually verified for correctness.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a computer capable of running Unity and profiling tools. Lab computers are available with all required software. Personal Jira/tracking tool accounts will be provided for the course.
